title: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine
authors: Neeraj K. Surana, Dennis L. Kasper

items.subtopic: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease
items.title: Role of the Microbiota in Human Physiology and Disease
items.content: The human microbiota, comprising bacteria, viruses, fungi, and Archaea, plays a crucial role in maintaining normal physiological processes. This complex interaction between human cells and microorganisms challenges traditional notions of 'self' by depicting humans as holobionts.
keyPoints: Microbiota impacts various body systems including intestinal disorders, metabolic functions, autoimmune diseases, and psychiatric conditions.
Changes in the microbiota are associated with susceptibility to infections and pharmaceutical therapy efficacy.
The exact mechanisms linking microbe-disease associations remain unclear; they may represent biomarkers or causal relationships.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease

items.subtopic: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease
items.title: Microbiota's Influence on Disease Susceptibility and Treatment Outcomes
items.content: Alterations in the microbiota have been linked to a wide array of diseases, influencing not only disease susceptibility but also treatment outcomes. Understanding these interactions is key to developing clinically meaningful interventions.
keyPoints: The microbiota's role extends beyond pathogenicity to include significant effects on health and disease states.
Disease-associated changes in the microbiota may serve as biomarkers or have causal relationships with diseases.
Research into host-commensal interactions is crucial for translating knowledge into clinical interventions.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY
items.title: The Evolution of Microbiome Research: From Leeuwenhoek to Modern Studies
items.content: Microbiome research has evolved significantly since Antony van Leeuwenhoek's initial observations in 1683. His pioneering work laid the groundwork for understanding microbiota, despite being initially dismissed. Over centuries, major projects like the Human Microbiome Project have advanced our comprehension of the microbiota's role in health and disease.
keyPoints: Antony van Leeuwenhoek first explored human microbiota in 1683.
The Human Microbiome Project (HMP) has catalogued bacteria at multiple body sites.
Research questions from over three centuries ago remain relevant today.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY
items.title: The Role of Commensal Bacteria in Human Health
items.content: The significance of commensal bacteria was recognized late in the 19th century, with Pasteur suggesting their necessity for survival. Despite initial skepticism, germ-free animal studies confirmed their importance. Metchnikoff's early 20th-century hypothesis on probiotics and aging highlighted potential therapeutic applications.
keyPoints: Pasteur suggested animals couldn't survive without microbes in 1885.
Germ-free animals disproved Pasteur's preconceived ideas in 1912.
Metchnikoff proposed that specific beneficial organisms could alter clinical outcomes.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY
items.title: Taxonomic Resolution in Microbiome Studies
items.content: Understanding taxonomy is crucial for interpreting microbiome studies. Only five bacterial phyla dominate the human microbiome, and early research focused on broad taxonomic levels like phylum. Modern advancements now allow strain-level comparisons, enhancing insights into host-commensal interactions.
keyPoints: Five dominant bacterial phyla in the human microbiome: Actinobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes, Fusobacteria, Proteobacteria.
Early studies compared microbial abundance at the phylum level.
Current analytical pipelines enable strain-level comparisons for detailed insights.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA
items.title: Anatomic Site vs. Interindividual Variation in Microbiota Composition
items.content: The composition of the human microbiota is more influenced by the anatomic site than by individual differences, although there is significant interindividual variation at any given body site.
keyPoints: Microbial populations are characterized across 15–18 body sites in healthy Western adults.
Samples from a specific body region (e.g., skin) are more similar to each other than to samples from different regions (e.g., stool), even within the same individual.
Interindividual variation is notable; for example, Bacteroidetes abundance in stool ranges from ~10% to >90% among individuals.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · OVERVIEW OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.subtopic: OVERVIEW OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA
items.title: Functional Redundancy and Conservation of Microbial Gene Content
items.content: Despite substantial differences in microbial composition, the functional properties of the microbiota are conserved across different individuals and body sites due to significant functional redundancy.
keyPoints: The functional capacity of the microbiota is similar across people and body sites, assessed via metagenomic data identifying gene pathways.
Microbial communities maintain core metabolic functions despite compositional differences.
Species like Staphylococcus epidermidis (93% in nares) and Escherichia coli (61% in stool) are prevalent at specific body sites.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · OVERVIEW OF THE HUMAN MICROBIOTA

items.subtopic: THE MICROBIOTA BY THE NUMBERS
items.title: Human Microbiota vs. Human Cells: Understanding Proportions
items.content: The human body hosts a vast number of microorganisms, with recent estimates suggesting that there are approximately 1.3 times more bacterial cells than human cells. This means humans are about 56% 'bacterial' by cell count. However, when considering viruses, fungi, and Archaea, microbes likely constitute over 90% of the total cellular content in the body.
keyPoints: Initial estimates suggested a tenfold greater number of bacteria than human cells.
Current studies suggest ~1.3 times more bacterial cells than human cells.
Humans are approximately 56% 'bacterial' by cell count.
Including viruses, fungi, and Archaea, microbes likely make up >90% of total cellular content.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · THE MICROBIOTA BY THE NUMBERS

items.subtopic: THE MICROBIOTA BY THE NUMBERS
items.title: Diversity and Genetic Potential of the Human Microbiota
items.content: The human microbiota is highly diverse, with over 10,000 different bacterial species identified across the body. An individual typically harbors between 500–1000 bacterial species at any given time, including 100–200 in the gut alone. Despite this diversity, only a limited number of bacterial phyla are found at each specific body site. The microbiota's genetic potential far exceeds that of the human genome, with an estimated >2,000,000 genes compared to ~20,000 in humans.
keyPoints: >10,000 different bacterial species exist within the human microbiota.
An individual typically harbors 500–1000 bacterial species at any time.
The gut alone contains 100–200 bacterial species.
Microbiota's genetic potential includes >2,000,000 genes compared to ~20,000 in humans.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · THE MICROBIOTA BY THE NUMBERS

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)
items.title: Genetic Influence on Microbiota Composition
items.content: Host genetics have a small but statistically significant effect on the microbiota's composition. Certain taxa, such as Christensenella species, are more heritable than others. While environmental factors play a larger role, genetic predisposition can indirectly affect disease susceptibility through alterations in the microbiota.
keyPoints: Genetics studies show a small but statistically significant effect on microbiota composition.
Christensenella species have higher heritability among taxa.
Environmental factors are more prominent modulators of the microbiome than genetics.
Genetic variation in immune-related genes can alter susceptibility to autoimmune diseases via changes in the microbiota.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)
items.title: Age-Related Changes in Microbiota
items.content: Microbial exposure may begin prenatally, and factors such as delivery mode and feeding method significantly influence early microbiota development. Post-birth, the infant's microbiota undergoes a succession process, becoming more adult-like by age 2–3 years. After this period, fecal microbiota remains stable until old age, where notable differences emerge.
keyPoints: Microbial exposure may begin in utero with bacterial DNA found in placentas and amniotic fluid.
Delivery mode (vaginal vs cesarean) and feeding method (breast milk vs formula) are major determinants of early microbiota.
Infant microbiota undergoes succession, resembling adult microbiota by age 2–3 years.
Fecal microbiota is generally stable after age 2–3 but shows differences in the elderly (>80 years), with increased Bacteroides and Eubacterium species.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)
items.title: Dietary Impact on Microbiota Composition
items.content: Diet is a strong determinant of microbiota composition, with significant shifts observed in response to dietary changes. Short-term macronutrient availability can cause rapid fluctuations in the fecal microbiota, while long-term patterns lead to stable compositions. Dietary interventions show that the microbiota reverts to its original state upon resumption of normal diet.
keyPoints: Diet strongly influences gut microbiota composition.
Short-term dietary changes cause rapid and reproducible fluctuations in fecal microbiota.
Vegetarian diets increase microbes metabolizing plant polysaccharides (e.g., Roseburia species).
Animal-based diets increase bile-tolerant organisms (e.g., Alistipes, Bilophila).
Microbiota reverts to its original state after dietary interventions and resumption of normal diet.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 1)

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)
items.title: Antibiotics' Impact on Gut Microbiota
items.content: Antibiotics significantly alter the gut microbiota by depleting antibiotic-sensitive strains and even some resistant ones. This effect is mediated through the loss of 'keystone' species crucial for maintaining microbial community structure.
keyPoints: Ciprofloxacin treatment results in a loss of roughly one-third of bacterial taxa, despite its limited activity against anaerobes.
Microbiota recovery post-antibiotic treatment varies among individuals; most recover within 4 weeks, but some changes last up to 6 months.
Repeated antibiotic courses lead to less predictable microbiota recovery and fewer individuals returning to baseline.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)
items.title: Lifestyle Influences on Microbiota
items.content: Household living arrangements, pets, and geographic location significantly influence the human microbiota. These factors contribute to microbial similarities among household members and changes in microbiota composition based on rural or urban settings.
keyPoints: Skin and fecal microbiotas are more similar among household members than between different households.
Presence of a dog increases similarity in skin microbiotas; cats and young children do not have the same effect.
Rural living leads to distinct fecal microbiota compared to urban environments, with significant shifts observed when moving countries.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)

items.subtopic: INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)
items.title: Circadian Rhythms and Microbiota
items.content: The human microbiota exhibits a circadian rhythm that is synchronized with the host's biological clock, affecting spatial localization, species abundance, and metabolite secretion. Disruptions in these rhythms can influence susceptibility to infections and contribute to metabolic diseases.
keyPoints: Microbial circadian oscillations are linked to the host's circadian clock and affect infection susceptibility.
Disruption of circadian rhythms in the host leads to similar disruptions in the microbiota.
Alterations in microbial oscillations have been associated with metabolic disease development.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · INFLUENCES ON THE MICROBIOTA (part 2)

items.subtopic: THE HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS
items.title: Why Microbial Exposure Reduces Autoimmune/Atopic Disease Risk
items.content: The hygiene hypothesis suggests that early-life exposure to microbes is crucial in preventing autoimmune and atopic diseases. This theory emerged from observing an inverse correlation between microbial exposure and the incidence of these conditions, proposing that such exposures help establish a balanced immune response.
keyPoints: Inverse correlation between microbial exposure and autoimmune/atopic disease incidence
Hygiene hypothesis proposed in 1989 to explain prevention of allergic diseases by early-life infections
Concept evolved to include genetic susceptibilities and the impact of 'westernization' on reduced microbial exposures
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · THE HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS

items.subtopic: THE HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS
items.title: How Western Lifestyle Affects Immune Development
items.content: The hygiene hypothesis posits that modern 'westernized' lifestyles, characterized by decreased exposure to diverse microbes, contribute to increased susceptibility to inflammatory and autoimmune diseases. This is due to a lack of early-life microbial exposures necessary for developing a balanced immune system.
keyPoints: Westernization leads to reduced microbial exposure
Lack of specific early-life microbial exposures can result in immune response dysregulation
Hypothesis now extends beyond atopic diseases to other inflammatory/autoimmune conditions
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · THE HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)
items.title: Microbial Diversity and Disease
items.content: Disease states are often associated with a reduction in microbial diversity, both in terms of alpha and beta diversity. This loss can be measured by the decrease in the number of species or operational taxonomic units and a reduction in microbial relatedness.
keyPoints: Diseases typically show less diverse microbiotas than healthy controls.
Alpha diversity is often decreased as seen by fewer operational taxonomic units (OTUs).
Beta diversity also decreases, indicating reduced microbial relatedness.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)
items.title: Inflammation and Microbial Abundance Changes
items.content: States of inflammation are linked to an increase in the relative abundance of Enterobacteriaceae and a decrease in Lachnospiraceae. These changes suggest specific microbial shifts associated with inflammatory processes.
keyPoints: Inflammation increases Enterobacteriaceae abundance.
Lachnospiraceae abundance decreases during inflammation.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)
items.title: Challenges in Microbiome Research
items.content: Most microbiome studies are correlational, making it difficult to establish causality. Animal models, particularly gnotobiotic mice, help explore causal relationships by controlling for host genetics and environmental factors.
keyPoints: Microbiome studies often face the 'chicken or egg' issue of correlation vs. causation.
Gnotobiotic mouse models are crucial for studying microbial effects on disease pathogenesis.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 1)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)
items.title: Microbiome's Role in Obesity
items.content: Obesity is linked to the gut microbiota, with studies showing that it influences energy extraction from diet, fatty acid absorption, lipid metabolism gene expression, and hepatic lipogenesis. Gnotobiotic mice resistant to obesity highlight this role. Human data are mixed; a meta-analysis of 10 studies found no consistent relationship between Bacteroidetes/Firmicutes ratio and obesity but noted ~2% lower diversity in obese individuals.
keyPoints: Gut microbiota impacts host metabolism, affecting body weight and adiposity.
GF mice resistant to diet-induced obesity demonstrate the microbiota's role.
Human studies show mixed results; meta-analysis found no consistent Bacteroidetes/Firmicutes ratio link but ~2% lower diversity in obesity.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)
items.title: Microbiome and Malnutrition
items.content: Malnutrition, particularly kwashiorkor, is associated with a microbiologically immature fecal microbiota. Studies in Malawian twins and Bangladeshi children show that specific bacterial species can prevent growth impairments when administered to mice or humans.
keyPoints: Kwashiorkor linked to an immature microbiota resembling younger children's.
Transplantation studies in gnotobiotic mice confirm the microbiome's causal role in poor weight gain.
Five bacterial species can prevent growth impairments when administered as a cocktail.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)
items.title: Microbiota and Inflammatory Bowel Disease
items.content: IBD, including ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease, is suggested to result from an immune response to a dysbiotic microbiota in genetically susceptible individuals. Antibiotic treatment can reduce disease severity, and fecal diversion prevents Crohn's relapses.
keyPoints: Genes account for ~20% of IBD susceptibility, many related to host-microbe interactions.
Antibiotic treatment reduces clinical severity of IBD.
Fecal stream diversion prevents Crohn's disease relapses.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)
items.title: Microbiome Diversity and Specific Bacterial Taxa in IBD
items.content: Patients with Inflammatory Bowel Disease (IBD) typically exhibit reduced alpha and beta diversity in their fecal microbiota compared to healthy controls. Notably, Clostridium clusters IV and XIVa are generally diminished, including specific bacteria like F. prausnitzii from cluster IV, which is often underrepresented in Crohn's disease patients' stool samples.
keyPoints: Reduced alpha and beta diversity in IBD patients' fecal microbiota
Clostridium clusters IV and XIVa generally reduced in IBD
F. prausnitzii from Clostridium cluster IV often underrepresented in Crohn's disease stool samples
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)
items.title: Role of Bacteriophages and Mycobiota in IBD Pathogenesis
items.content: Emerging evidence suggests that Caudovirales bacteriophages may play a role in the pathogenesis of IBD, potentially reflecting underlying dysbiosis. Additionally, dysregulation of the fungal component (mycobiota) is linked to disease severity, affecting the mucosal immune system.
keyPoints: Caudovirales bacteriophages implicated in IBD pathogenesis
Dysregulation of mycobiota linked to IBD disease severity
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)
items.title: Microbial Influence on Cardiovascular Disease Pathogenesis
items.content: Microbes, such as Chlamydia pneumoniae and Porphyromonas gingivalis, are implicated in the pathogenesis of atherosclerosis. Higher antibody titers to C. pneumoniae are observed in patients with cardiovascular disease, and this bacterium is found within atherosclerotic lesions.
keyPoints: Chlamydia pneumoniae associated with higher antibody titers in cardiovascular disease
C. pneumoniae presence within atherosclerotic lesions
Porphyromonas gingivalis also implicated in atherosclerosis pathogenesis
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 3)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)
items.title: Role of TMAO in Atherosclerosis and Thrombosis
items.content: Trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) is linked to atherosclerotic heart disease, with higher levels observed in omnivores due to dietary intake of carnitine and choline. Animal studies show that gut microbiota transfer can increase TMAO levels and atherosclerosis. Plasma TMAO levels predict thrombosis risk, but specific bacterial taxa associated with this risk are not more abundant in high-risk individuals.
keyPoints: TMAO is linked to atherosclerotic heart disease.
Higher TMAO levels in omnivores due to red meat, eggs, and dairy intake.
Plasma TMAO levels predict thrombosis risk (myocardial infarction, stroke) in >4000 patients study.
Complexity of microbiota dynamics; specific bacteria not more abundant in high-risk individuals.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)
items.title: Microbiota's Influence on Cancer Therapy Efficacy
items.content: Specific microbiota members can enhance or reduce the efficacy of cancer therapies. Anti-PD-L1 therapy effectiveness is influenced by bacteria like Bifidobacterium, Faecalibacterium, and Akkermansia. FMT from responders to non-responders has shown potential benefits in anti-PD-1 therapy. Conversely, intratumoral bacteria can metabolize chemotherapeutic agents, affecting drug resistance.
keyPoints: Specific microbiota members affect cancer treatment efficacy.
Bacteria like Bifidobacterium, Faecalibacterium, and Akkermansia influence anti-PD-L1 therapy.
FMT shows potential in enhancing response to anti-PD-1 therapy.
Intratumoral bacteria can metabolize chemotherapeutic agents, affecting drug resistance.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)
items.title: Microbial Signatures in Cancer Genomics
items.content: Reanalysis of TCGA data identified microbial signatures predicting clinical outcomes in cancer, though these findings are controversial due to potential computational errors. The complexity of bioinformatic pipelines and low microbial abundance in samples necessitate further validation.
keyPoints: Microbial signatures within tumor genomes may predict cancer outcomes.
Findings from TCGA data reanalysis are controversial.
Complexity of bioinformatic pipelines and low microbial abundance require further study.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 4)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)
items.title: Microbiome's Role in HSCT and GVHD
items.content: The microbiome significantly impacts hematopoietic stem cell transplantation (HSCT) outcomes, particularly concerning graft-versus-host disease (GVHD). Studies indicate that gut microbial diversity at the time of neutrophil engraftment correlates with lower mortality. Specific bacteria like Blautia species may protect against GVHD and related mortality.
keyPoints: GF mice developed less frequent and severe gut GVHD than wild-type mice in 1970s studies.
A higher bacterial diversity at time of neutrophil engraftment is associated with lower mortality.
Imipenem-cilastatin or piperacillin-tazobactam for neutropenic fever linked to increased GVHD-related mortality at 5 years.
Enterococcus species and other pathogens dominate (>30% abundance) in fecal microbiota post-allogeneic HSCT.
Blautia species abundance may protect against GVHD and mortality, though causality is still under investigation.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)
items.title: Microbiome Influence on Type 1 Diabetes Development
items.content: Environmental factors, including the microbiome, play a role in the development of autoimmune diseases like type 1 diabetes (T1D). A decrease in alpha diversity by ~25% occurs after seroconversion but before T1D diagnosis. Bacteroides species are more abundant in high-risk children, potentially affecting disease onset through altered bacterial LPS exposure.
keyPoints: 70% of T1D patients have HLA risk alleles, yet only 3-7% develop the disease, indicating environmental factors' role.
Alpha diversity decreases by ~25% after seroconversion but before T1D diagnosis in high-risk children.
Bacteroides species are more abundant in high-risk children compared to low-risk ones, potentially linked to altered LPS exposure.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)
items.title: Study Approaches for Microbiome and Disease Relationships
items.content: Two approaches are highlighted for studying microbiome-disease relationships: longitudinal studies of high-risk individuals to map temporal changes, and careful participant selection to uncover meaningful associations. These methods aim to explore host-microbe interactions and potential causality in disease development.
keyPoints: Longitudinal studies can provide insights into host-microbe relationships by mapping microbiome changes with disease onset.
Associations identified may reflect preclinical disease rather than direct causality.
Careful participant selection can reveal meaningful associations for experimental verification.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 5)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)
items.title: Role of Microbiota in Rheumatoid Arthritis
items.content: Rheumatoid arthritis (RA) pathogenesis is influenced by the microbiota, with evidence showing that germ-free mice do not develop RA symptoms and antibiotic treatment reduces RA development. P. copri has been identified as a biomarker for newly diagnosed RA but does not indicate causality.
keyPoints: Germ-free mice do not develop RA in models.
Antibiotic treatment mitigates RA development in mice.
P. copri is a biomarker of new-onset RA, not chronic or psoriatic arthritis.
Lack of strict concordance between P. copri presence and RA argues against causality.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)
items.title: Microbiota's Link to Multiple Sclerosis
items.content: Multiple sclerosis (MS) has a minor genetic component, with environmental factors playing a larger role. Evidence suggests gut microbiota influences MS, as shown in murine models where germ-free and antibiotic-treated animals have reduced disease incidence.
keyPoints: Genetics plays a minor role; ~30% of monozygotic twins both develop MS if one has it.
Germ-free and antibiotic-treated mice show reduced MS incidence and severity.
Clinical studies link microbial changes with MS, but causality is not established.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)
items.title: Microbiome Influence on Atopic Diseases
items.content: The microbiome is linked to the progression of atopic diseases, which often follow a sequence from atopic dermatitis to food allergy, asthma, and allergic rhinitis. The gut microbiota may modulate these conditions.
keyPoints: Atopic disease prevalence is increasing with more severe presentations.
Progression typically follows: AD -> food allergy -> asthma -> allergic rhinitis.
Microbiome linked to modulation of atopic diseases.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 6)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)
items.title: Role of Skin Microbiota in Atopic Dermatitis
items.content: Atopic dermatitis (AD) involves immune dysfunction and a dysbiotic skin microbiota characterized by increased Staphylococcus aureus and reduced bacterial diversity. Restoration of normal microbial diversity, particularly through the use of lantibiotic-producing coagulase-negative staphylococci (CoNS), can decrease S. aureus abundance.
keyPoints: AD is marked by greater abundances of Staphylococcus aureus and reduced bacterial diversity.
Restoration of normal microbial diversity reestablishes immune homeostasis.
Lantibiotic-producing CoNS (e.g., S. epidermidis, S. hominis) can reduce S. aureus abundance when applied topically.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)
items.title: Microbiome Influence on Asthma Development
items.content: Early-life microbial exposures, such as colonization with S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, and M. catarrhalis, are associated with an increased risk of persistent wheeze and asthma by age 5. Lower abundances of Lachnospira, Veillonella, Faecalibacterium, and Rothia at 3 months also correlate with higher asthma risk.
keyPoints: Early-life colonization with S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae, M. catarrhalis linked to persistent wheeze/asthma by age 5.
Increased eosinophilia and IgE levels in children colonized with these organisms at 4 years of age.
Lower abundances of Lachnospira, Veillonella, Faecalibacterium, Rothia at 3 months associated with increased asthma risk.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)
items.title: Potential for Microbiome Modulation in Asthma Prevention
items.content: Transplantation of stool samples from infants at risk for asthma into gnotobiotic mice showed that early-life modulation of the microbiome can reduce airway inflammation, suggesting a preventive strategy against asthma.
keyPoints: Stool transplantation from 3-month-old children at risk for asthma into gnotobiotic mice causes significant airway inflammation.
Pre- and postnatal exposure to specific bacterial strains (F. prausnitzii, V. parvula, R. mucilaginosa, L. multipara) inhibits airway inflammation in mice.
Reduction in neutrophil numbers observed in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of treated mice.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 7)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)
items.title: Colonization Resistance and Its Role in Preventing Enteric Infections
items.content: The concept of colonization resistance highlights the protective role of normal intestinal microbiota against enteric pathogens. This protection is primarily attributed to anaerobic gram-positive organisms, which prevent pathogen colonization and subsequent disease.
keyPoints: Colonization resistance prevents disease by inhibiting pathogen colonization via normal intestinal microbiota.
Protection largely relies on anaerobic gram-positive organisms.
The relevance of the gut-lung axis in humans is still under investigation.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)
items.title: Fecal Microbiota Transplantation for Recurrent Clostridioides difficile Infection
items.content: FMT is an effective treatment for recurrent CDI, with a clinical cure rate of 85–90%. It leverages the restoration of microbiome diversity to counteract dysbiosis caused by antibiotic exposure.
keyPoints: 15–30% of patients treated for CDI experience recurrence.
FMT has an 85–90% success rate in treating recurrent CDI.
C. scindens is associated with protection from CDI, suggesting potential microbiome-based therapies.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)
items.title: Microbiome Dynamics in Vibrio cholerae Infection
items.content: V. cholerae infection leads to a loss of microbiota diversity and altered composition, which are reversible with treatment. Ruminococcus obeum has been identified as inhibiting V. cholerae growth through quorum-sensing mechanisms.
keyPoints: Vibrio cholerae infection causes a marked loss of microbial diversity.
Microbiome recovery post-treatment mirrors healthy infant microbiota maturation.
Ruminococcus obeum restricts V. cholerae colonization via AI-2 quorum-sensing.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 8)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)
items.title: Role of Microbiota in Viral Infections
items.content: The microbiota can influence the pathogenesis and immune response to viral infections. Commensal bacteria are crucial for inducing type I interferons, a defense mechanism against viruses, and modulating cellular physiology to inhibit viral replication.
keyPoints: Bacterial component of microbiota is required for some viral infections' pathogenesis.
Antibiotic therapy can reduce disease severity in animal models of viral infection.
Commensal bacteria induce type I interferons against viruses.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)
items.title: Microbiota's Influence on HIV Pathogenesis and Acquisition
items.content: The microbiota affects HIV pathogenesis, immune activation, and acquisition risk. Differences in the fecal microbiota correlate with systemic inflammation markers in HIV patients. Vaginal microbiota composition influences HIV acquisition risk and antiviral efficacy of treatments.
keyPoints: Microbial environment influences HIV disease severity.
Intestinal immune system regulates HIV-induced immune activation.
Lactobacillus species reduce, while Prevotella bivia increases, HIV acquisition risk.
Tenofovir gel's efficacy varies with vaginal microbiota composition.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)
items.title: Microbiota's Impact on Vaccine Response
items.content: The microbiota influences the immune response to vaccines, serving as an adjuvant for certain vaccine types. Differences in fecal and nasal microbiota are associated with altered vaccine responses.
keyPoints: Microbiota affects immune response magnitude to vaccines.
Fecal and nasal microbiota linked to vaccine response variations.
Nonadjuvanted viral subunit vaccine responses rely on the microbiota.
Antibiotic treatment impacts influenza vaccine efficacy in individuals with low preexisting immunity.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 9)

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF MICROBIOME-MEDIATED EFFECTS
items.title: Why Small Sample Sizes in Microbiome Studies May Yield False Positives
items.content: Clinical studies examining the microbiome often have small sample sizes (often <100) and compare numerous variables, such as each bacterial species. This can lead to inadequate statistical power, increasing the risk of false-positive results.
keyPoints: Sample size in clinical microbiome studies is often <100.
Studies may not be adequately powered due to small sample sizes.
Comparing numerous variables increases the risk of false positives.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MECHANISMS OF MICROBIOME-MEDIATED EFFECTS

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF MICROBIOME-MEDIATED EFFECTS
items.title: How Animal Models Help Establish Causality in Microbiome Studies
items.content: Animal models are crucial for demonstrating causal relationships between specific microbes and disease phenotypes, as microbiome-wide association studies often yield numerous correlations without clear causality.
keyPoints: Microbiome-wide association studies result in many bacterial taxa correlated with diseases.
Animal models are essential to establish causality between microbes and specific phenotypes.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MECHANISMS OF MICROBIOME-MEDIATED EFFECTS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Role of Microbiota in Immune System Development
items.content: The microbiota is essential for the proper development, education, and maintenance of the immune system. Germ-free (GF) animals exhibit an immature and underdeveloped immune system, highlighting the necessity of a host-specific microbiota for normal immune maturation.
keyPoints: Microbiota co-evolved with its host to support immune system development.
Germ-free animals have an immature immune system.
Host-specific microbiota is crucial for normal immune maturation.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Impact of Early-Life Microbial Exposure
items.content: Early-life microbial exposures are critical and must occur within a time-sensitive window to ensure proper neonatal immune development. Failure to have these exposures during this period can lead to long-lasting physiological consequences.
keyPoints: Maternal transfer of microbially-targeted antibodies and metabolites augments neonatal immune development.
Early-life microbial exposures must occur within a time-sensitive window.
Failure to meet early exposure requirements can result in long-term physiological impacts.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)
items.title: Immunomodulatory Effects of B. fragilis
items.content: Bacteroides fragilis polysaccharide A (PSA) is a well-studied molecule that influences disease outcomes by inducing regulatory T cells and protecting against colitis and multiple sclerosis in mouse models.
keyPoints: PSA has a unique zwitterionic structure with both positive and negative charges.
PSA is recognized by Toll-like receptor 2 on antigen-presenting cells.
PSA induces IL-10 producing regulatory T cells (Tregs) to restrain inflammation.
PSA confers protection against experimental colitis and MS in mouse models.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Role of SCFAs in Immune Modulation and Treg Induction
items.content: Short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs) produced by gut microbiota from dietary fibers are crucial for immune modulation, particularly in inducing regulatory T cells (Tregs). However, the specific SCFA responsible for this effect is not definitively identified, with propionate, acetate, and butyrate all implicated. The mechanism involves inhibition of histone deacetylase leading to increased Foxp3 expression, although SCFAs are not essential for Treg induction by certain bacteria.
keyPoints: SCFAs include propionate, acetate, and butyrate.
Elevated cecal levels of SCFAs correlate with Treg induction in wild-type mice colonized with specific bacteria.
SCFA production is not critical for Treg induction by all organisms; other mechanisms may exist.
SCFAs also enhance epithelial barrier function, affect cell proliferation, regulate metabolism, and provide energy to colonocytes.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Microbiota Metabolism of Host-Derived Molecules
items.content: The gut microbiota metabolizes host-derived molecules like bile acids, influencing lipid and carbohydrate metabolism and immune responses. Bile acid profiles are altered by microbial dysbiosis, correlating with various diseases such as hepatic inflammation, colorectal cancer, and impaired motility. These alterations suggest a role in Treg homeostasis but causal relationships require further investigation.
keyPoints: Bile acids are metabolized into deconjugated and secondary forms by gut bacteria.
Microbially produced bile acid profiles affect lipid/carbohydrate metabolism and immune responses.
Altered bile acid profiles correlate with hepatic inflammation, colorectal cancer, and impaired motility.
Bile acid metabolites influence colonic Treg homeostasis.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)
items.title: Diverse Microbial Metabolites from Host-Derived Molecules
items.content: The gut microbiota can metabolize various host-derived molecules, such as taurine and histamine, affecting immune responses like IL-18 secretion. Additionally, inosine produced by Bifidobacterium pseudolongum enhances the efficacy of checkpoint blockade inhibitors in mice. These examples highlight the potential for numerous bacterial metabolites to impact health and disease, though clinical relevance is still under investigation.
keyPoints: Taurine increases IL-18 secretion via NLRP6 inflammasome; histamine, spermine, and putrescine suppress it.
Inosine enhances checkpoint blockade inhibitor efficacy in mouse models.
Microbial metabolism of host-derived molecules regulates their levels and effects on the immune system.
Clinical relevance of these bacterial metabolites remains to be fully established.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)
items.title: FMT as a Therapeutic Intervention for CDI
items.content: Fecal Microbiota Transplantation (FMT) has shown remarkable efficacy in treating Clostridioides difficile Infection (CDI), with an average clinical cure rate of approximately 85%. This therapy involves transplanting stool from a healthy donor to the patient, aiming to correct microbial dysbiosis.
keyPoints: Average clinical cure rate for CDI is ~85%
FMT can be administered via various routes: nasogastric tube, nasoduodenal tube, colonoscopy, enema, or oral capsules
Lower-gastrointestinal administration has a slightly higher cure rate than upper-gastrointestinal methods
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)
items.title: Safety and Adverse Effects of FMT
items.content: FMT is generally safe with transient adverse effects such as altered gastrointestinal motility, abdominal cramps, and bloating. These typically resolve within 48 hours. However, there have been cases of antimicrobial-resistant pathogen transmission leading to mortality.
keyPoints: Common adverse effects include constipation or diarrhea, abdominal cramps, and bloating
Adverse effects generally resolve within 48 hours
Cases of antimicrobial-resistant pathogens transmitted by FMT have led to mortality
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)
items.title: Expanded Applications and Challenges of FMT
items.content: Beyond CDI, FMT is being investigated for various conditions including IBD, obesity, and anxiety. While results are promising for CDI, outcomes for other diseases like IBD have been mixed, potentially due to variations in recipient characteristics or FMT administration details.
keyPoints: >500 trials investigating FMT for multiple indications as of July 2024
Mixed results for conditions other than CDI, such as IBD
Variability may be due to differences in recipients, donor material, and FMT logistics
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 1)

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Challenges and Future Directions of Microbiome-Based Therapies
items.content: Microbiome-based therapies, such as FMT, face challenges in standardization due to variability among donors and recipients. Safety concerns and unclear mechanisms further complicate their use. However, advancements are leading towards more refined treatments like bacterial cocktails or specific microbial products.
keyPoints: FMT is difficult to standardize across populations.
Safety concerns exist with FMT.
Mechanisms of action for FMT are not well understood.
Future therapies may include refined bacterial cocktails, single strains, or microbial metabolites.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Evolution of Probiotic Research and Its Implications
items.content: Probiotic research has a complex history with many strains tested for various diseases. Meta-analyses generally find the data insufficient to support these regimens, often due to selection based on safety rather than biological relevance. Future probiotics aim to be rationally chosen based on specific disease mechanisms.
keyPoints: Meta-analyses have not convincingly supported current probiotic regimens.
Probiotics are often selected for presumed safety over biological plausibility.
Future probiotics will focus on specific commensal organisms linked to disease pathogenesis.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE

items.subtopic: MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE
items.title: Microbiome Research and Its Impact on Disease Treatment
items.content: The field of microbiome research is shifting from an 'us-versus-them' mentality to recognizing the symbiotic relationship between humans and microbes. This understanding has implications for autoimmune and inflammatory diseases, with future strategies potentially involving modulation of microbial ecology.
keyPoints: Microbiome research has evolved from viewing microbes as enemies to partners in health.
Loss of host-microbe interactions may contribute to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases.
Future treatments may involve modulating beneficial or deleterious microbes.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · MOVING MICROBIOME SCIENCE FROM BENCH TO BEDSIDE (part 2) | 3842 PERSPECTIVE

items.question: Which scientist suggested that animals could not survive without common microbes?

items.subtopic: HISTORICAL PERSPECTIVE | A PRIMER ON TAXONOMY
items.question: What was Élie Metchnikoff's hypothesis regarding aging?
options: Aging is caused by a lack of vitamins.
Aging is caused by toxic bacteria in the gut, mitigated by lactic acid-producing bacteria.
Aging is accelerated by exposure to sunlight.
Aging is due to genetic mutations.
items.explanation: Élie Metchnikoff hypothesized that aging was caused by toxic bacteria in the gut and could be mitigated by lactic acid-producing bacteria like Lactobacillus species.

items.question: Which of these bacterial phyla is NOT a dominant member of the human microbiome?
items.explanation: The five dominant members of the human microbiome are Actinobacteria, Bacteroidetes, Firmicutes, Fusobacteria, and Proteobacteria. Cyanobacteria is not one of them.

The time of day when samples are collected.

It remains quite similar despite differences in microbial composition.

items.question: What does the hygiene hypothesis suggest about the relationship between microbial exposure and atopic diseases?

Increased microbial exposure increases the incidence of atopic diseases.
There is no correlation between microbial exposure and atopic diseases.
Only genetic factors influence the development of atopic diseases.

Disease states are typically associated with decreased alpha and beta diversity.
Disease states show no significant change in microbial diversity compared to healthy individuals.

items.question: Why is it difficult to determine causality in microbiome studies related to disease?

Due to the confounding 'chicken or egg' issue in case-control study designs.
As all studies use gnotobiotic mice, which do not reflect human conditions.
Because stool samples perfectly represent mucosa-adherent organisms.

The ease of collecting stool samples compared to biopsy samples.
Stool samples have no microbial 'noise' that affects the study results.
Biopsy samples are more informative for all scientific questions.
items.explanation: Stool is often used as a surrogate because it is relatively easy to collect compared to biopsy samples, despite some limitations in representing mucosa-adherent organisms.

items.explanation: These animal experiments allow for controlled investigation of microbial components, helping to identify causal roles played by microbes in disease pathogenesis.

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)

items.subtopic: RELATIONSHIP BETWEEN THE MICROBIOTA AND SPECIFIC DISEASE STATES (part 2)
items.statement: Antibiotic treatment can reduce the clinical severity of inflammatory bowel disease.

items.statement: Organisms with choline utilization genes were more abundant in animals at greater risk for thrombosis.

items.subtopic: MECHANISMS OF MICROBIOME-MEDIATED EFFECTS

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 1)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.subtopic: REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)
items.statement: Microbially produced bile acid metabolites influence colonic Treg homeostasis.
items.explanation: The passage confirms that microbe-produced bile acid metabolites do influence colonic Treg homeostasis.
items.reference: The Human Microbiome in Health and Disease · REGULATION OF THE IMMUNE SYSTEM (part 2)

items.statement: FMT has been proven effective in large controlled studies across all indications.
